title: Triple Bunk Staggered Beds
summary: A staggered three-tier bunk built almost entirely from 1x4s and bolted together so it knocks down — an ambitious, lumber-heavy build for a shared kids' room.
description:
This is a serious project: three staggered bunk beds stacked and offset so the built-in ladder steps climb through the frame. It's framed from an enormous quantity of 1x4s with 2x3 slat supports, and — crucially — it's bolted rather than glued so it can be disassembled to move through doorways and up stairs. Read the whole plan and stage your cuts before you start; there are well over a hundred pieces.

The method is panel-based. You build flat panels — end pieces, inner cross sections, front/back double sections, and front/back single/ladder sections — each with rails marked at a consistent 12-3/4" spacing down the verticals. The staggered geometry comes from where each panel's rails and steps land, so mark carefully from the top down using the given dimensions; a mismeasured panel throws off every bolt hole that references it.

Two details make it safe and reusable. Bolt holes are drilled about 1" below each bed rail (and below the top safety rail) so the nuts sit in safe positions directly under the cross rails, out of the way of sleepers. And the slats are fastened to each other rather than screwed to the supports, so they hold their spacing as a mat but lift out for disassembly. This is a build to take slow — and with a bunk this tall, the top safety rails are non-negotiable.

tools.note: Well over a hundred repeat crosscuts — a stop block saves your sanity.
For the bolt holes through the verticals.

materials.name: 1x4 board, 8 ft
materials.note: The bulk of the frame, rails, steps, and slats.

materials.name: 3" bolts with nuts and washers
materials.note: Bolt holes drilled ~1" below each rail so nuts sit safely under the cross rails.

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: Cut twelve full-height legs at 70-3/4", four single-end legs at 45-1/4", four hanging end support at 43-1/2", four hanging mid-bunk step at 29", six feet at 3-1/2", two single-end feet at 29", two double-end filler at 47-1/2", two hanging support at 22", two hanging bottom end at 1-3/4", 28 filler between steps and rails at 9-1/4", two ladder top at 12-3/4", six side rails at 59-1/2", six foot-section rails at 21", two top safety rails at 61-1/4", four fourth step & mid-bunk side rail at 22-3/4", two second-mid step at 42", 18 end rails at 39", twelve inner-vertical supports at 2-1/2", 27 slats at 40-1/4" and four foot-section slats at 39" from the 1x4. Cut two lower slat supports at 54", two upper slat supports at 34-3/4" and 14 cross slat supports at 15-1/2" from the 2x3. Allowing about 1/8" for each saw cut, that comes to 51 8 ft 1x4 and five 8 ft 2x3 — 51 of the 52 boards the materials list declares, so there is one spare 1x4 and no spare 2x3 at all. Set a stop block on the saw before you start: the 28 fillers at 9-1/4", the 27 slats at 40-1/4" and the 18 end rails at 39" are 73 of the 149 1x4 pieces on their own, and a stop block is the only realistic way to make them identical. Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw.

steps.title: Build the end panels
steps.body: Build the two end pieces — one full height, one for the mid-bunk end. On the verticals, mark from the top down at 12-3/4", 25-1/2", and 38-1/4", plus 51" and 63-3/4" on the full-height vertical, with the last mark 7" from the bottom. These marks set every rail height, so measure them precisely.

steps.title: Build the inner cross sections
steps.body: Build the two inner cross sections — one full height, one hanging. Rails sit the same 12-3/4" apart as the ends. Each cross section has one rail installed flat, which becomes a mattress support.

steps.title: Build the front and back double sections
steps.body: Build the front and back double sections as mirror images of each other. Mark the full-height verticals from the top at 12-3/4", 25-1/2", 38-1/4", 51", and 63-3/4" (last mark 7" up), aligning to the end pieces' rails.

steps.title: Build the single/ladder sections
steps.body: Build the front and back single/ladder sections, also mirror images. Mark the verticals at the same spacing as the double sections. On these, the main verticals sit on the outside of the rails so the ladder steps read cleanly.

steps.title: Drill the bolt holes
steps.body: Drill every connection hole about 1" below the bed rail it serves, plus one below each top safety rail, so the nut and washer end up tucked directly under a rail instead of sitting proud where a child can catch a knee on it. Clamp the two panels that will bolt together in their finished relationship and drill both at once — the hole has to line up through both faces, and a hole drilled from one side alone and then transferred by eye will not. That accounts for all 36 bolts: 32 of the 3" and four of the 2-1/2". Put a washer under the head and under the nut on every one, so the bolt does not crush the pine as it draws up and then go slack a month later. Before you drill anything, check each hole position against the 12-3/4" rail marks you laid out on the panels — a bolt hole is not something you get to move.

steps.title: Connect the slats and assemble
steps.body: Lay the 2x3 slat supports in place first — the two 54" lower supports, the two 34-3/4" upper supports and the fourteen 15-1/2" cross supports, eight to the middle bunk, four to the upper and two to the lower as the cut list notes. Then build each bunk's slats into a mat: fasten the slats to one another rather than screwing them down to the supports, so the mat holds its spacing but still lifts straight out — screwing them to the supports would kill the knockdown feature that this whole bolted design exists for. The 27 40-1/4" slats serve the three main sleeping areas and the four 39" foot-section slats fill the shorter foot bays. Stand the end panels, the two inner cross sections and the front and back sections up and draw them together with the bolts, working from the bottom bunk upward and checking the frame stands plumb before you fully tighten anything. Fit both 61-1/4" top safety rails last, and do not leave them off — on a bunk this tall they are the difference between a bed and a fall.

images.alt: Staggered triple bunk bed built from 1x4 lumber
